mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the full scope of motion pictures as an art form, including classification, all component arts (acting, directing, editing, camera, animation), production processes, and the industry; also addresses documentary and propaganda functions under C.2 and the historical development of the medium for C.3.

Covers the motion-picture industry as part of the global entertainment industry (C.1) and the educational and propagandistic functions of mass entertainment media (C.2), though not specific to film production or aesthetic questions.

Pioneered synchronized sound and Technicolor animation (A.2.e), produced feature-length animated films illustrating motion-picture production (A.3), and built a paradigmatic entertainment industry conglomerate (C.1).

Directly covers animation as a technology and creative method within film production, addressing traditional cel animation, CGI, and stop-motion as distinct branches of A.2.e (use of technology, animation, special effects) and their role in the production process (A.3).
Addresses the live acting tradition from which motion-picture acting is distinguished (A.2.b) and represents the primary art form whose interrelation with motion pictures is examined under B.

Covers screenplay and scriptwriting as a literary form (A.2.a, cross-referenced from 621) and represents one of the arts whose integration with film is treated under B (literature supplying source material, narrative, and genre conventions for motion pictures).

Embodies the silent-film acting tradition and the distinction of screen acting from stage acting (A.2.b), exercised total directorial and editorial control (A.2.c, A.2.d), and helped establish Hollywood as a global industry institution (C.1).

The camera and photochemical/electronic image-capture technology that photography developed are foundational to the cinematographic apparatus described under A.2.e, making photography directly relevant to the technological dimension of the creative process.

Television is a competing and complementary medium that shaped the economic structure of the motion-picture industry (C.1) and became a major channel for the distribution and function of moving-image media in education and culture (C.2).

The camera is the central technological instrument of motion-picture production addressed under A.2.e, covering lens optics, aperture, shutter, and both film and digital sensor technology directly applicable to cinematography.

Held fundamental patents in motion picture technology (kinetoscope, kinetograph) directly relevant to A.2.e, and his industrial research model contributed to establishing the early motion-picture industry (C.1).

Situates motion pictures within the mass-culture industry (C.1) and examines film as a vehicle for shaping popular values and attitudes, relevant to C.2 (functions of motion pictures as media of education and cultural influence).

Modernism's influence on cinematic montage and the self-conscious rejection of theatrical narrative conventions is directly relevant to the nature and classification of motion-picture art (A.1), situating film within the broader early-20th-century aesthetic revolution.
Dance is one of the arts whose interrelation with motion pictures is examined under B, especially in musical films, dance films, and choreography adapted for the screen.
Music is central to the interrelation of arts in motion pictures (B), providing synchronized sound and scoring, while sound recording technology addressed in A.2.e is shared with music production.

The Nazi use of film as propaganda — including Riefenstahl's state-commissioned documentaries — is the most historically prominent example of motion pictures deployed as media of propaganda, directly relevant to C.2.
